macro_events.event_summary: Two ships were attacked in the Strait of Hormuz per UK maritime authorities, raising Middle East shipping/oil supply risk despite the extended US-Iran ceasefire.
primary_drivers: Oil supply risk premium on ~20% of global seaborne crude transiting Hormuz
Tanker rates spike from rerouting and war-risk insurance premiums

beneficiaries.rationale: Frontline is a pure-play crude tanker operator (VLCC/Suezmax); Hormuz attacks spike spot tanker rates and war-risk insurance, directly lifting day rates on its fleet.
International Seaways operates crude and product tankers with significant Middle East Gulf loading exposure; rerouting and risk premiums flow directly to spot rates.
Scorpio Tankers is the largest pure-play product tanker operator; Hormuz disruption tightens product tanker market as refined products from Gulf refiners reroute.
DHT Holdings is a pure-play VLCC operator entirely spot/time-charter exposed to Middle East Gulf loadings — highest beta to Hormuz rate spikes.
Integrated major with large upstream book; brent crude premium from Hormuz risk lifts near-term realizations, and limited MidEast upstream exposure means more upside than downside risk.
Lockheed makes PAC-3/THAAD interceptors and munitions replenished after Gulf escalation; any Iran-linked escalation reinforces FY defense budget tailwind.
Raytheon supplies Patriot missiles and naval air defense (SM-series) — direct beneficiary of any renewed Gulf maritime defense tasking and restock orders.
Cheniere is largest US LNG exporter; Hormuz risk threatens Qatari LNG flows (~20% of global supply), making US Gulf LNG a premium substitute — TTF spread widens.

losers.rationale: Delta has higher jet fuel sensitivity than peers and limited fuel hedging; Brent spike directly pressures CASM-ex and Q2 margin guide.
United's long-haul international network (including Gulf routings) is exposed to both jet fuel spike and potential airspace reroutes over Iran/Iraq.
Southwest's all-domestic model still makes it one of the most fuel-cost-sensitive US carriers; unhedged exposure to crude spike compresses near-term EPS.
Carnival burns ~3M barrels/year of marine fuel; bunker prices track Brent and Middle East cruise itineraries face cancellation/rerouting risk.
FedEx Express jet fuel is a top-3 cost line; Brent spike pressures margins and Middle East hub (Dubai) transit faces elevated risk surcharges.

headlines: Two ships attacked in Strait of Hormuz, UK says, after U.S. extends Iran ceasefire

macro_events.event_summary: Micron is lobbying US Congress to tighten export controls on semiconductor manufacturing equipment sales to Chinese memory chip rivals (CXMT, YMTC).
primary_drivers: Export control tightening reduces Chinese memory capacity buildout
Reduced supply from Chinese DRAM/NAND competitors supports global memory pricing
Revenue hit to US/allied equipment makers with China exposure

beneficiaries.rationale: Direct instigator and primary beneficiary — slowing CXMT's DRAM ramp protects Micron's ~23% global DRAM share and supports HBM/DDR5 pricing power. CXMT has been flooding the low-end DRAM market in 2025-26, pressuring commodity DRAM ASPs.
Western Digital's NAND/flash business competes directly with YMTC; restrictions on tools to Chinese NAND makers reduce supply pressure in an already oversupplied NAND market.
Pure-play NAND flash (post-WDC spin); same dynamic — constrained YMTC capacity supports NAND pricing which is the entire P&L.

losers.rationale: Applied Materials derives ~30%+ of revenue from China; tighter controls on memory tool sales directly cuts TAM. Memory equipment is a core segment and China memory fabs have been a growth driver.
Lam Research is the most memory-equipment-exposed US WFE vendor (etch/deposition for 3D NAND and DRAM) with ~30% China revenue exposure. Direct revenue hit if memory tool restrictions expand.
KLA's process control tools ship to Chinese memory fabs; less exposed than LRCX/AMAT but still material China revenue (~25%) at risk from expanded memory-specific controls.
Already restricted on EUV; additional DUV restrictions to Chinese memory customers would further cut China service/DUV revenue (was ~20-30% of shipments). US-listed ADR directly exposed.
ACM Research sells wet-clean tools primarily to Chinese fabs including CXMT/YMTC; secondary-sanctions or customer-capex risk if their key customers can't get complementary US tools to build out fabs.

headlines: Micron pushes US Congress to crack down on chip tool sales to Chinese rivals, sources say

macro_events.event_summary: Rift Helium lists on London's AIM market, highlighting tightening global helium supply amid geopolitical risks to Russian and Qatari production.
primary_drivers: Tightening global helium supply raising spot prices
Increased investor attention on helium-exposed producers
Supply security premium for non-Russian/Qatari sources

beneficiaries.rationale: Air Products is one of the largest global helium distributors with long-term sourcing contracts; tighter supply lets them reprice contracts higher and widen margins on the helium segment.
Linde is a top-3 global industrial gas player with significant helium sourcing and distribution exposure; benefits from pricing power in a constrained market.
Not helium — ignore. Replaced by: sentiment-driven peer rotation into small-cap helium names often pulls in US-listed exploration plays.

losers.rationale: Semiconductor lithography tools and fab operations consume significant helium for cooling; sustained helium price increases raise operating costs for ASML customers and indirectly for ASML's own testing, though pass-through is likely. Weak second-order link.

headlines: Rift Helium lists on AIM as geopolitical risk tightens global supply
